# Illinois CS Places 28 Faculty on CITL List of Teachers Ranked as Excellent By Their Students

Source: Siebel School of Computing and Data Science (Illinois CS), 3/27/2023,
by Aaron Seidlitz. For the Fall 2022 semester, the Center for Innovation in
Teaching and Learning (CITL) ranked these UIUC CS faculty as excellent.

## Ranked "Outstanding" (the highest honor on the list)
- Jeff Erickson — CS 473: Algorithms
- Ryan Cunningham — CS 498: Law & Policy Issues in CS
- Timothy Chan — CS 598: Fine-Grained Algorithms
- Aishwarya Ganesan — CS 598: ML for Systems

## The other 24 faculty ranked as Excellent
- Ram Alagappan — CS 598: Cloud Storage Systems: Theory & Practice
- Lawrence Angrave — CS 341: System Programming
- Chandra Chekuri — CS 498: Algorithms for Big Data
- Katie Cunningham — CS 500: Current Topics in Computing Education Research
- G. Carl Evans — CS 225: Data Structures
- Wade Fagen-Ulmschneider — CS 340: Introduction to Computer Systems AND CS 107: Data Science Discovery
- Christopher Fletcher — CS 563: Advanced Computer Security
- Yael Gertner — CS 401: Accelerated Fundamentals of Algorithms I AND CS 491: Excursions in Computing
- Saugata Ghose — CS 233: Computer Architecture
- Jiawei Han — CS 598: Text Mining: A New Paradigm
- Geoffrey Herman — CS 233: Computer Architecture AND CS 500: Current Topics in Computing Education Research
- Nan Jiang — CS 542: Statistical Reinforcement Learning
- Dakshita Khurana — ECE 407: Cryptography
- Robin Kravets — ECE 439: Wireless Networks
- Colleen Lewis — CS 491: Intro to Inclusive Terminology
- Charith Mendis — CS 598: ML for Compilers & Architecture
- Radhika Mittal — CS 598: High-Speed/Programmable Networks
- Klara Nahrstedt — CS 537: Advanced Topics in Internet of Things (IoT)
- Luke Olson — CS 598: Scientific Machine Learning
- Yongjoo Park — CS 511: Advanced Data Management
- Brad Solomon — CS 199: Honors for Data Structures (CS 225)
- Elahe Soltanaghai — CS 598: Smart Cities, Homes, & Beyond
- Luther Tychonievich — CS 418: Interactive Computer Graphics
- Tiffani Williams — CS 400: Accelerated Fundamentals of Computing I AND CS 491: Excursions in Computing

"Our faculty's commitment to excellence in teaching computer science shines
through in many ways," said Department Head Nancy M. Amato. "We teach a
demanding subject that constantly evolves," said Mahesh Viswanathan, Associate
Head for Academics. "Our professors maintain an impressive command of the
subject matter, and relay that to our excellent students in exciting ways."

# The Actual Masters Experience (USA)

Author: Suvodeep Sinha (AI @ Intel | CS @ UIUC) — Medium, May 22, 2024

I just completed 2 semesters at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign as a
Masters student in Computer Science. It was a roller coaster ride with extreme
highs and lows. Through this blog I share insight into the educational degree
itself — the workload, difficulty, and instructor quality.

Note: in UIUC, 400-level courses are open for graduate students and undergrads
(for a lesser credit option) and 500-level courses are graduate-only courses.

## Semester I

### CS 444 Deep Learning for Computer Vision
This was the most challenging course that I had taken this semester. It covered
basic topics from Linear Regression to advanced Transformer and Diffusion
models. Structure: 5 coding assignments, 1 project (equivalent of 3
assignments), 2 exams. Although the assignments were well documented,
implementing them in code was challenging. The exams were math intensive and
quite mentally exhausting but that is expected from a grad Machine Learning
class. The professor and the course material were extremely helpful for
understanding basics and served as a strong foundation for the multiple ML
engineering interviews that I gave. A must-take if you're interested in this
field broadly.

### CS 445 Computational Photography
I took this course to test whether I wanted a deep dive into computer vision and
3D image problems. This class introduces several classical CV problems and does
not involve any form of Machine Learning. The course structure was exactly the
same as CS 444. The coding assignments were very practical/hands-on. Any errors
faced taught the student to solve the problem rather than just ask ChatGPT. I
would rate this a well-balanced course for learning and workload. Beneficial if
you like to research problems specific to CV or aim at an industry role in CV.

### CS 410 Text Information Systems
I had little to no background in text retrieval or NLP and wanted to start
bottom up rather than jumping to LLMs. This course covered text retrieval, text
mining, search engines, recommendation systems and much more. Structure: 15
weekly quizzes, 3 coding assignments, 1 paper review, 1 project, 2 exams.
Although this may seem a lot on paper, it wasn't the case. In-depth
understanding of the concepts was enough for a lot of them. The class was well
paced, beginner friendly, and the content was very easy to follow. This was my
most interactive class and I recommend it to anyone who doesn't know how to
begin with textual data.

### CS 591 CS Colloquium
Not a traditional class but a 1-credit optional course that required students to
attend seminars/talks or conferences throughout the semester and write a brief
report about the major learnings and intriguing questions from those talks.

## Semester II

### CS 568 User-Centered Machine Learning
To better understand design principles, I wanted a course sharing topics in the
HCI realm. We focused on researching AI systems that are easy to use, adapt to
individual needs, and improve user satisfaction. Structure: 2 research paper
readings weekly, 1 final project (45% of the grade). The course was heavily
research-focused. I recommend this course to anyone wanting to experiment with
HCI+NLP fields with a focus on language models.

### CS 510 Advanced Information Retrieval
The advanced version of CS 410 — quite rigorous but very rewarding. It covered
advanced topics in information retrieval: mixture models, statistical models,
and ways to obtain Big Text data. Special focus was given to LLMs and their
applications. Structure: 4 quizzes, 3 assignments, 1 experimental assignment on
LLM and a large codebase, 1 paper/toolkit review, 1 exam, 1 project. Having
taken CS 410 was an added advantage.

### CS 412 Introduction to Data Mining
This course is taken by a lot of undergrads as it covers many topics of
statistics in depth (bias-variance, PCA, EDA, overfitting). It helped with the
basics of data mining: preparing data, storing it, finding patterns, and
grouping similar data. Structure: 3 assignments, 2 coding assignments, 3 exams,
1 project. I enjoyed the coding assignments the most — implementing a
pre-existing technique/library from scratch (e.g., the Apriori Algorithm). A
good intro to data or ML class if you're new to the field.

## Last Words
I hope I gave some idea of the average workload expected as a graduate student.
My case was biased for CS but most STEM fields are similar. Balance the
internship/job hunt, find ways to motivate yourself, and take regular breaks.

# My Journey through the Online MCS-DS Programme (r/UIUC_MCS)

Posted by u/palunel, 6 years ago, on r/UIUC_MCS.

I completed my Masters in Computer Science in Data Science in December last
year. It was a real wild ride! Lots of work but I have not had such a rewarding
learning experience in decades. I kept track of the effort each course took.

## Time to complete each course (hours)
- CS 410 Text Information Systems: 121:28 (Fall 2018)
- CS 425 Distributed Systems: 131:37 (Fall 2018)
- CS 498 Cloud Computing Applications: 154:58 (Spring 2019)
- CS 498 Applied Machine Learning: 204:55 (Spring 2019)
- CS 498 Data Visualization: 98:05 (Summer 2019)
- CS 513 Theory and Practice of Data Cleaning: 46:22 (Summer 2019)
- CS 598 Advanced Bayesian Modeling: 146:34 (Fall 2019)
- CS 598 Practical Statistical Learning: 229:45 (Fall 2019)

Workload per semester: Fall 2018 = 253:07, Spring 2019 = 359:52, Summer 2019 =
144:28, Fall 2019 = 376:19. Total duration of program: 475 days; days spent
studying: 326; total time studying: 1133:47 hours; average per study day: 3:28;
average per week: 16:42 hours. I worked full time while doing this, so it was a
work of passion.

## OP's opinions on each course (from the comments)

### CS 410 Text Information Systems
A course with real practical application. Enjoyed it very much and left feeling
comfortable that I can implement anything from sentiment analysis to search
algorithms with state-of-the-art techniques. What was difficult? Prof Zhai is
clearly an accomplished researcher but I often struggled to follow his English.
What helped? The textbook was very useful and lectures were pretty much verbatim
from it. The practical applications were also encouraging.

### CS 425 Distributed Systems
Fascinating insights for someone who does not come from a cloud computing
background. Really enjoyed Prof Gupta's lectures and it sparked an interest in
things "cloud." What was difficult? The C++ programming assignment was quite
challenging. The exams were also challenging — long and in depth; you really
needed to understand the content to do well.

### CS 498 Cloud Computing Applications
A bit of an odd course. The main problem is that content needs to be updated
faster than it can be, since it talks to what systems are available in the wild.
At times I was not sure the lecturers really understood some of the tech
presented. In the end I enjoyed it very much. What was difficult? Sometimes I
felt a disconnect between assignments and lectures; some assignments were really
tough. What helped? Plenty of resources on the web. (A commenter notes CCA was
later overhauled and now has a lot of programming, primarily Java but Python is
allowed, with many MPs/machine problems — so it is practical.)

### CS 498 Applied Machine Learning
I really looked forward to this one. However, the lectures were not all that
useful with a lot of very complicated math that was (thankfully) never tested.
It was all about the assignments, which at times had me pulling my hair out. The
assignments were great though and covered a really wide field of ML with deep
dives into algorithms. What was difficult? I never really got what Prof Forsyth
was on about — he made it sound like everything was obvious, but nothing really
was. What helped? The forums.

### CS 498 Data Visualization
Useful and a nice intro into d3.js and JavaScript in general. Well presented
with nice assignments. Not the most difficult of the assembly.

### CS 513 Theory and Practice of Data Cleaning
Not sure about this one. I spent only a fifth of the time of the most onerous
course on this one. Not sure I will use any of the tools presented. The concepts
are fine and important but perhaps not worth all the credits. What was
difficult? Nothing really. It was good to have this low-intensity course in the
middle of the program.

### CS 598 Advanced Bayesian Modeling
Possibly one of the best courses of the program. The work was really challenging
for someone with no real stats (let alone Bayesian stats) background. I found
the math challenging, and unlike AML, we were actually tested on it. Prof Park
was an absolute highlight of the course — great and very supportive. The
curriculum was well thought out and well presented, and the assignments talked
directly to the work covered. What was difficult? Pretty much everything; the
exams were challenging. The constant use of case studies put theory into
context and made the assignments doable and enjoyable.

### CS 598 Practical Statistical Learning
An odd one. I felt the lecturer Prof Liang managed to make fairly easy topics
sound very complicated, and Prof Zhu got some criticism on the forums for
support of concepts that were unclear. The content was good — one of the most
important of the program — but it was not presented and supported the way I
would have expected. I found two textbooks exceptionally helpful and learned
more through the YouTube videos of Prof Tibshirani and Hastie. I did enjoy the
two projects at the end. What was difficult? A disconnect between lectures and
assignments. What helped? The forums.
